header.title: Riscore
header.subtitle: Invest smarter, risk safer — real-time explainable risk scoring on live market data

placeholder.us: Search US stocks — Apple, TSLA, NVDA…
placeholder.cn: Search China A-shares — Moutai, Ping An Bank, 600519, 000001…

emptyState.heading: Search any stock to see its risk
emptyState.body: Type a company name or ticker above. Scores update live using real market data.

data.title: Real market data
data.body: Live US / China A-share prices with daily snapshot fallback.

explain.title: Explainable by construction
explain.body: Five visible lenses; every number carries a plain-language reading.

validated.title: Walk-forward validated ML
validated.body: 56 stocks × 5 years; accuracy published, weight capped at 15%.

honest.title: Honest about limits
honest.body: Weaknesses documented. Descriptive statistics — never advice.

labelExplanation.LOW: Quieter than this stock’s own normal — smaller day-to-day moves, and it hasn’t fallen as far as it usually does.
labelExplanation.MODERATE: About normal for this stock. Nothing unusual compared with how it usually behaves.
labelExplanation.HIGH: Bumpier than this stock’s own normal — either it’s swinging more each day, or it has fallen further than it typically does.
labelExplanation.EXTREME: About as wild as this stock gets — it’s moving near the most extreme levels seen in its own recent history.

keyFactors.title: Key Factor Contributions
keyFactors.flooredNote: Left the score unchanged — a low reading here isn’t a safety margin.

explainer.intro: We compare this stock only with its own past two years — never with other stocks. 0 means “about as calm as this stock ever gets”, 100 means “about as wild as it ever gets”. A high score doesn’t mean it’s a bad stock, and it doesn’t mean it’s about to fall — it means it’s moving around more than this particular stock usually does.

volatility.label: Volatility
volatility.plain: Realised volatility. A percentile blend of 21-day (40%) and 63-day (35%) annualised return standard deviation, plus 63-day downside deviation (25%), which counts only downward moves. Each input is ranked against this stock’s own ~2-year history.

tail.label: Tail Risk
tail.plainShort: How ugly the rare bad days get
tail.plain: Left-tail severity. Blends 21-day 95% Conditional VaR (expected shortfall, 35%) and 95% Value at Risk (25%) with 63-day return skewness (20%) and kurtosis (20%) — the shape of the return distribution, not just its width.

drawdown.label: Drawdown
drawdown.plainShort: How far it can fall at worst
drawdown.plain: Peak-to-trough decline. Blends 63-day maximum drawdown (45%), the current drawdown from the running peak (35%), and drawdown duration — sessions spent below that peak (20%).

sensitivity.label: Market Sensitivity
sensitivity.plainShort: Does it follow the whole market?
sensitivity.plain: Beta against the market benchmark (SPY or CSI 300, by listing), estimated over a rolling 63-day window as the covariance of returns divided by the benchmark’s variance. 1.0 = moves one-for-one with the benchmark.

liquidity.label: Liquidity
liquidity.plainShort: How easy it is to sell
liquidity.plain: Trading frictions. Blends the 21-day Amihud illiquidity ratio — absolute return per unit of dollar volume (50%) — with volume volatility (30%) and the dollar-volume level (20%).

glossary.volatility: Annualised realised volatility: the standard deviation of daily returns over the trailing window, scaled by √252. Direction-agnostic — it measures dispersion, not drift, so a stock trending steadily upward can still read high.
glossary.var95: Value at Risk (95%), 21-day window: the loss threshold that the worst 5% of daily returns exceed, taken as an empirical quantile rather than assuming a normal distribution. It bounds the typical bad day, not the worst case — Conditional VaR (under Tail Risk) measures what lies beyond it.
glossary.beta: Beta over a rolling 63-day window: the covariance of this stock’s returns with its benchmark, divided by the benchmark’s variance. 1.0 = one-for-one with the benchmark; below 1 damped, above 1 amplified. It captures co-movement, not causation.
glossary.rsi: Relative Strength Index (14) — Wilder’s momentum oscillator: 100 − 100/(1 + average gain ÷ average loss) over 14 sessions. Above 70 is conventionally “overbought”, below 30 “oversold”. A description of momentum, not a forecast.

readings.title: What this number actually means
readings.disclaimer: This describes what already happened — it is not a prediction, and not advice.

vol.low: The price has been moving in small steps lately — a fairly smooth ride.
vol.normal: Day-to-day moves are about what you’d expect from an ordinary stock.
vol.elevated: It’s been jumping around more than most stocks — bigger moves in both directions.
vol.high: Very bumpy lately. Daily moves have been much bigger than a typical stock’s.

var95.mild: On a bad day it has typically lost about this much — small, as these things go.
var95.moderate: On a bad day it has typically lost about this much — a middling amount.
var95.elevated: On a bad day it has typically lost about this much — enough to sting.
var95.severe: On a bad day it has typically lost about this much — a heavy one-day hit.

beta.negative: It’s been going the opposite way to the market — up on days the market fell, and vice versa.
beta.defensive: It moves less than the market, so market-wide drops land more gently here.
beta.inline: It moves roughly in step with the market.
beta.amplified: It exaggerates the market — when the market moves 1%, this tends to move more.
beta.high: It exaggerates the market strongly, so market-wide swings hit this much harder.

rsi.oversold: A lot of selling recently. Below 30 is what people call “oversold” — it describes what just happened, not what comes next.
rsi.weak: Sellers have had the upper hand lately, but nothing extreme.
rsi.neutral: Buyers and sellers have been fairly evenly matched.
rsi.firm: Buyers have had the upper hand lately, but nothing extreme.
rsi.overbought: A lot of buying recently. Above 70 is what people call “overbought” — it describes what just happened, not what comes next.

factor.low: Quieter than this stock’s own normal.
factor.elevated: Higher than this stock’s own normal — one of the main things pushing the score up.
factor.high: Near the highest this stock has ever been — a main reason the score sits where it does.

sensitivity.low: Barely follows the market right now. That cushions market-wide selloffs — and it also means this one tends to sit out market-wide rallies.
sensitivity.moderate: Follows the market about as closely as it usually does — it will feel broad moves in both directions.
sensitivity.elevated: Tracking the market more tightly than usual, so a market-wide drop lands harder here than it normally would.
sensitivity.high: About as tied to the market as it ever gets — broad selloffs hit this near their full force.

liquidity.low: Trades very easily at the moment — cheap to get in and out of. That’s a convenience, not a reason the position is any safer to hold.
liquidity.moderate: Trades about as easily as it usually does.
liquidity.elevated: Harder to trade than usual — moving a position costs more in price impact than it normally would.
liquidity.high: About as hard to trade as it ever gets: thin volume, so exiting can move the price against you.

window.title: {ticker} · {period} window
window.note: These figures cover the selected window only. The risk score above is ranked against roughly two years of history no matter which timeframe is selected, so it stays comparable when you switch — it isn’t stale.

board.title: Your watchlist
board.subtitle: Risk movement since the previous reading
board.note: A rising score means risk increased, a falling score means it eased — shown the same way for every market, because a risk score is not a price. Readings come from stored daily snapshots, not a live quote.
board.exportCsv: Export readings as CSV (caveat line included)

alerts.title: Risk movement
alerts.subtitle: Notable changes on your watchlist
alerts.empty: No notable movement since you last checked.
alerts.note: Triggered by a large change or a band crossing. Descriptive of stored readings — not a forecast or a recommendation.

compare.note: Each score is relative to that stock’s own history, so a higher number means "more turbulent than usual for itself" — not "riskier than the stock next to it". Descriptive statistics, not a recommendation.
compare.radarNote: Each axis is that stock's percentile against its own history — overlap shows shape, not objective safety.
compare.priceNote: Each line is rebased to 0% at the window start so different price levels share one axis.

riskNote.percentile: Score reflects this stock's risk relative to its own historical distribution (and market sensitivity vs. {benchmark}) — it is not a probability of loss, default probability, or investment recommendation.
riskNote.fused: Score blends this stock's risk percentile relative to its own history ({pct}%) with a walk-forward-validated ML estimate of its 20-day severe-drawdown probability ({ml}%), plus market sensitivity vs. {benchmark} — it is not a probability of loss, default probability, or investment recommendation.

stressTest.intro: A “what if” exercise: if a crash like one of these happened again, where would this stock’s score likely end up? We take how this particular stock behaves today and replay those past conditions against it. Nobody is predicting these events will repeat — it’s a way to see how much room there is to get worse.
stressTest.narrative: If {label} conditions recurred, this stock's risk score would move from {baseline} to {stressed} ({delta}).

regimeSignals.intro: Context, not scoring. These read the market backdrop and the stock’s own price structure: is volatility running hotter than the market priced, does it behave like a cyclical or a defensive name, has it run up hard while getting choppy, and what has its candle chart printed lately. None of it moves the risk score above — it has not been backtested against future losses yet, so it is shown as information rather than folded into the number.

patterns.label: Candlestick patterns
patterns.none: No reversal patterns in the last {days} sessions.

mlSignal.intro: A computer model’s rough guess at the odds of a 10%-or-worse fall within the next month of trading. It’s one small input (15%) to the headline score, not the whole thing. Be aware of its weak spot: it misses more real falls than it catches, so treat a low number as “no signal here”, not as “this is safe”.
mlSignal.waterfallNote: Bars are in log-odds — the only scale where SHAP contributions truly add up; red pushes the drawdown probability up, green pushes it down, and “other features” covers everything outside the top list. Attribution shows association, not causation.

outcomes.toggle: What happened at this risk level before
outcomes.intro: Looking back over the last two years: on days when this stock sat in each risk band, what actually happened over the following 20 trading days? These are historical frequencies for this stock — how often it ended higher or lower, the typical range of outcomes, and how often a 10%+ drop or 10%+ jump occurred along the way.
outcomes.noData: This stock hasn’t traded at this risk level in the lookback window.
outcomes.takeaway: Note what the score actually shifts: higher risk bands widen the range of outcomes in BOTH directions — bigger drops and bigger jumps become more common — rather than simply making a fall more likely. That is what the risk score measures: turbulence, not direction.
outcomes.disclaimer: Historical frequencies for this stock only — past patterns, not probabilities of what happens next, and not investment advice.

auth.nicknameHint: 2–30 characters — shown on your posts instead of your email
auth.agreeNotAdvice: I understand risk scores and all community posts are data-driven reference only, not investment advice
auth.agreeCommunityRules: I agree to only post stock risk-analysis content here — no trading directives, political discussion, solicitation, or off-topic content
auth.consentNotice: Your nickname (not your email) is shown publicly on your posts. The site admin can see your nickname, the email you sign up with, and basic usage data (which features you use and when) — used only for data analysis and keeping the site secure.
auth.switchToSignUp: Don't have an account? Sign up
auth.switchToSignIn: Already have an account? Sign in

watchlist.title: My Watchlist
watchlist.empty: No saved stocks yet — tap the star on any card to save it here.

community.title: Community Risk Analysis
community.disclaimer: The risk score is computed from objective market data. Everything in this community section is other users’ personal opinion, including accuracy rates derived from votes — use your own judgment, don’t treat it as advice.
community.feedEmpty: No analysis posted yet — be the first to share your take.
community.leaderboardEmpty: No ranked analysts yet — accuracy needs a minimum number of votes to appear here.
community.bodyPlaceholder: Share your read on this stock’s risk…
community.scopeHint: 💡 Risk-analysis observations only — no trading directives, political content, or off-topic posts.
community.postDisclaimer: Posting means this is a risk-analysis discussion, not investment advice.
community.signInToPost: Sign in to share your own analysis.
community.opinionNote: Community observations are individual users’ own opinions — not Riscore’s view, and not investment advice.
community.beFirst: No analysis yet for this stock — be the first to share your take.

moderation.trading_directive: Posts can’t contain buy/sell calls or price targets — describe the risk data instead.
moderation.solicitation: Posts can’t contain contact info, group invites, or promotions.
moderation.abuse: That language isn’t allowed here — keep it civil.

report.prompt: Why are you reporting this post?

community.precheckWarn: This looks like it may contain a trade directive or a guarantee — posts like that are rejected by the content filter. Consider rephrasing as an observation.

reports.empty: No pending reports — the community queue is clear.

welcome.title: Welcome to Riscore
welcome.body: Riscore turns a stock’s recent price and volatility behavior into one explainable 0–100 risk score, plus the individual signals behind it — so you can quickly see how turbulent a stock is acting right now. This short tour explains what each number describes, and what it deliberately does not: Riscore visualizes risk statistics; it never gives investment advice.

score.title: The Risk Score (0–100)
score.body: Your at-a-glance read: LOW / MODERATE / HIGH / EXTREME compares the stock to its own recent history, not to other stocks. A jump into HIGH or EXTREME describes turbulence — bigger swings than this stock’s own normal — and nothing more. It is not a buy/sell signal, and Riscore never suggests what to do about it; for decisions, consult a licensed financial adviser.

breakdown.title: What’s driving the score
breakdown.body: Open "What does this score mean?" to see the five ingredients — Volatility, Tail Risk, Drawdown, Market Sensitivity, Liquidity — and their weights. Knowing which category is elevated tells you what kind of turbulence the number is describing: elevated Liquidity and elevated Tail Risk are very different situations, even at the same headline score.

mlSignal.title: ML downside-risk signal
mlSignal.body: A secondary machine-learning estimate of the probability of a 10%+ drop in the next 20 trading days, with the specific factors (SHAP) that drove it. It’s a deliberately small (15%) input to the score — treat it as one more corroborating data point, not a standalone forecast; its recall is intentionally documented as low.

stressTest.title: Historical stress test
stressTest.body: Shows how this stock’s score would move if a past crisis (2008 / 2020 / 2022) repeated, based only on its own volatility/tail/drawdown profile. Use it for worst-case scenario planning — it’s not a prediction that any of these events will happen again.

metrics.title: Quant metrics at a glance
metrics.body: 30-day volatility, VaR 95%, Beta, and RSI — the numbers analysts check first. VaR 95% estimates a plausible bad-day loss; Beta tells you how much the stock amplifies market-wide moves; RSI flags short-term overbought/oversold momentum.

watchlist.title: Save stocks to your watchlist
watchlist.body: Sign in and tap the star on any card to track it here across visits — useful for monitoring how a position’s risk profile evolves over time instead of starting fresh every time you check it.

about.title: About Riscore — how it works
about.intro: Riscore turns live market data into one explainable 0–100 risk score per stock — a reading of how turbulently a stock is behaving versus its own history, with every input visible and every number explained in plain language. Built as a serious, transparent risk-analysis tool: no black boxes, no advice, no hype.

data.title: Live market data, resilient by design
data.body: US prices via the Twelve Data market-data API; China A-shares via akshare. Daily-refreshed snapshots keep scores available even when an upstream source throttles.

lenses.title: Five explainable risk lenses
lenses.body: Volatility, tail risk, drawdown, market sensitivity and liquidity — each measured as a percentile against the same stock’s own ~2-year history, then combined with regime-aware weights that adapt to overall market stress (VIX).

ml.title: Machine learning, kept on a leash
ml.body: A walk-forward-validated XGBoost model estimates the probability of a 10%+ drawdown in the next 20 trading days. It contributes a deliberately capped 15% of the headline score, and every prediction ships with its SHAP factor breakdown.

stress.title: Historical stress tests & outcome bands
stress.body: Every stock is replayed against 2008 / 2020 / 2022 crisis conditions, and each risk band shows what actually happened at that level in the past — real frequencies, not promises.

explain.title: Deterministic plain-language explanations
explain.body: Every metric’s reading comes from reviewed threshold tables — not from a language model — so the wording is auditable, consistent, and can never drift into advice.

about.validationIntro: The ML leg is evaluated walk-forward — trained only on data that came before what it is tested on, the same way it would run live:

weight.label: capped ML share of the score

about.honesty1: We publish the model’s weaknesses too: its recall is documented as low — it misses more real drawdowns than it flags. Treat a low probability as “no signal here”, never as “safe”.
about.honesty2: The score describes the past and present — percentiles of observed behavior. It is not a prediction of future prices and never becomes one.
about.honesty3: When a data source is throttled or a model artefact is unavailable, the app says so and degrades visibly — it never silently invents numbers.

accounts.title: Accounts done properly
accounts.body: Passwords are bcrypt-hashed (never stored or logged in plain text) and sessions use signed JWTs. Banned accounts are locked out at every endpoint on their next request.

data.title: Minimal data, explicit consent
data.body: We store only what the product needs: email, nickname, watchlist, posts and votes. Sign-up requires itemized consent, and the privacy notice says exactly what the admin can see and why — data analysis and site security, nothing else. Nothing is sold or shared.

community.title: A moderated community
community.body: Posts are screened against scope rules (no trade directives, no solicitation, no abuse), any user can report a post, and reports go to an admin review queue with ban powers behind it.

transparency.title: Transparent by construction
transparency.body: The factor weights, thresholds, validation write-up and known limitations are documented openly — the same numbers this page quotes. If the docs and the app ever disagree, that’s a bug, not marketing.

about.responsible: Riscore is an educational risk-analysis tool. Scores are descriptive statistics about observed market behavior; community posts are individual users’ opinions. Nothing on this site is investment advice or a solicitation to trade — always use your own judgment.

footer.tagline: Explainable risk scoring on live market data.
footer.disclaimerBody1: Riscore is a quantitative risk visualization tool. We display statistical risk metrics based on historical market data.
footer.disclaimerAdviser: Always consult a licensed financial adviser before making investment decisions.
footer.privacyBody1: We store only what your account needs to work: your email, your hashed password, your watchlist, and any posts or votes you make in the Community board. We do not sell your data or share it with advertisers.
footer.privacyBody2: By creating an account and using this site, you consent to that data being processed to provide the service. You can delete your own posts at any time, or contact the admin below about closing your account.
footer.licenseBody1: Risk scores are computed from market data provided by Twelve Data (US) and akshare (China A-shares). This tool is for informational and educational purposes only — it is not investment advice, and nothing on this site is a solicitation to buy or sell any security.
footer.licenseBody2: Community analysis posts are the personal opinions of individual users, not the platform.
footer.contactBody: We don't publish a support email address — the way to reach the site admin is inside the app, through the Community board where the admin account is active.

replay.title: Simulated user journey replay
replay.banner: Simulated journey. These steps come from a research harness driven by developer-encoded behavioural assumptions — they are a hypothesis about a product risk, not a recording of a real person.
replay.intro: Each replay shows what one simulated user saw, what they understood or misread, and what they intended to do. Load a replay JSON produced by the simulation harness, or explore the bundled sample.
replay.invalidFile: That file is not a valid replay JSON.
replay.confidenceHidden: True data confidence was low - the product showed no confidence flag.

coldStart.title: Waking the free instance…
coldStart.body: This demo runs on a free Render instance that sleeps when idle. The first request after it naps takes roughly 60–100 seconds to boot. Once awake, everything responds normally.

capabilities.title: What actually runs on this deployment
capabilities.intro: This project is bigger than what a free instance serves. So that nothing here is overstated, every capability is labelled with where it actually runs — verified against this deployment, not assumed.
capabilities.liveNote: Running in production right now, on every score request.
capabilities.degradedNote: Upstream blocks datacenter IPs, so these fields come back empty here. The app degrades instead of failing — but the limitation is real, so it is stated rather than hidden.
capabilities.repoNote: Built and tested, but with no endpoint or UI on this site. Listed here so the site never implies more than it serves; each is verifiable from the repo.

items.composite: Percentile composite across five risk lenses
items.metrics: VaR, CVaR, beta, volatility, drawdown, liquidity
items.ml: XGBoost drawdown probability with SHAP attribution
items.vix: VIX and market-regime weighting — falls back to "calm" when unavailable
items.news: LLM news-risk extraction — no API key configured on this deployment
items.portfolio: Portfolio risk attribution (component VaR, HHI, effective-N)
items.governance: Model registry, lineage and model cards

capabilities.verifyHint: Verify any of these yourself from the repository:

learn.title: Learn — how to read a risk score
learn.intro: A short, plain-language guide for newer investors. Everything here explains what the numbers on this site describe, and what they deliberately do not. Nothing on this page is investment advice.
learn.tryBody: Drag the slider. The score is a percentile: it compares this stock against its own past two years, not against other stocks. A 70 does not mean a 70% chance of losing money — it means calmer than only 30% of its own history.
learn.notProb: This is the single most common misreading. A score of 80 is not an 80% chance of a loss. It is a ranking against this stock’s own history — nothing more.
learn.notCompare: Scores are self-relative, so comparing two different stocks’ scores tells you how unusual each is versus its own past — not which one is objectively safer.
learn.factorsBody: The score blends five lenses. Each is ranked against the stock’s own history, then weighted. Two of them (market sensitivity and liquidity) can raise the score but never lower it — a calm, easily-traded stock should not earn a discount for being boring.
learn.twoSidedNote: Can push the score up, never down — a calm, liquid stock earns no discount for it.
learn.stressTitle: 3. How to read a stress test
learn.stressBody: A stress test replays real historical conditions — 2008, 2020, 2022 — against this stock’s current behaviour, and asks where its score would sit under those conditions. It is a what-if exercise using past data. It is not a forecast, and nobody is predicting these events will recur.
learn.riskReturnTitle: 4. Risk is not the same as bad
learn.riskReturnBody: A high score means turbulence, not doom. Turbulence widens the range of outcomes in both directions — bigger falls and bigger jumps both become more likely. Whether that suits you depends on your own horizon and circumstances, which this site knows nothing about.
learn.riskReturnWarn: This site never tells you what to buy or sell, never sets price targets, and cannot judge whether something suits you. For decisions that matter, talk to a qualified adviser.
learn.glossaryBody: Plain definitions for the terms used across the site.

terms.riskScore: Risk score — a 0-100 percentile ranking this stock against its own recent history. Higher means more turbulent than its own normal. Not a probability, not a recommendation.
terms.percentile: Percentile — where today’s reading sits inside a sorted history. 90 means only 10% of the past two years was more extreme.
terms.volatility: Volatility — how much the price moves day to day, in either direction. It measures spread, not direction, so a steadily rising stock can still read high.
terms.downsideDeviation: Downside deviation — like volatility, but counting only the falls. Useful when you care about drops rather than movement in general.
terms.var: Value at Risk (VaR) — a threshold for a bad day. A 95% VaR of -6% means that on the worst 5% of days, losses exceeded 6%. It is not a maximum loss.
terms.cvar: Conditional VaR / Expected Shortfall — the average loss on those worst days, once VaR has already been breached. It describes how bad the bad days actually get.
terms.drawdown: Drawdown — how far the price has fallen from its most recent peak. A recovery only counts once the old peak is regained.
terms.maxDrawdown: Maximum drawdown — the deepest peak-to-trough fall within a window. A blunt measure of the worst stretch an investor would have sat through.
terms.beta: Beta — how much the stock moves with the wider market. Above 1 amplifies market moves; below 1 damps them. It is co-movement, not cause.
terms.liquidity: Liquidity — how easily the stock can be traded without moving its own price. Thin liquidity makes exiting expensive, especially in a panic.
terms.amihud: Amihud illiquidity — price impact per unit of trading volume. Higher means a given trade shifts the price more.
terms.skew: Skew — whether the tail of returns leans negative or positive. Negative skew means rare large falls are the bigger risk.
terms.kurtosis: Kurtosis — how fat the tails are. High kurtosis means extreme moves happen more often than a normal bell curve would suggest.
terms.garch: GJR-GARCH — a volatility model that lets falls raise future volatility more than equivalent rises do, matching how markets actually behave.
terms.har: HAR-RV — a volatility model that blends daily, weekly and monthly realised volatility, capturing how different horizons interact.
terms.impliedVolatility: Implied volatility — the volatility the options market is currently pricing in. A forward-looking expectation, not a measurement of the past.
terms.stressTest: Stress test — replaying historical crisis conditions against current behaviour to see where the score would move. Descriptive, not predictive.
terms.shap: SHAP — a method for attributing a model’s output across its inputs, showing which features pushed a prediction up or down. It shows association, not causation.
terms.calibration: Calibration — whether a model’s stated probabilities match observed frequencies. A well-calibrated 20% should happen about 20% of the time.
terms.breachRate: Breach rate — how often losses actually exceeded VaR. A 9% realised rate against a 5% target means the model understated tail risk.
terms.concentration: Concentration — how much of a portfolio’s risk sits in one position. Holding many names does not guarantee diversification if they move together.
terms.regime: Market regime — a coarse read of overall conditions (calm / elevated / panic), used to shift how much each factor counts.

tickerBar.scheduleNote: Session state from the exchange schedule and clock only — holidays are not modelled.

tech.title: Tech stack — what actually runs
tech.intro: Assembled from the repository's own dependency manifests, not aspiration. Every metric below is the repo's published number or a real configuration value.

react.name: React 18 + Vite
react.why: Component model for the card system; Vite for instant dev builds.

tailwind.name: Tailwind CSS
tailwind.why: Utility classes keep the dark theme consistent without a bespoke CSS layer.

recharts.name: Recharts
recharts.why: SVG charts — price history, risk bands, radar — with one declarative API.

i18next.name: i18next
i18next.why: EN / 简体 / 繁體 from one key tree, parity-tested in CI.

fastapi.name: FastAPI
fastapi.why: Typed Python API; the scoring pipeline is Python end to end.

sqlmodel.name: SQLModel + Alembic
sqlmodel.why: SQLite persistence with versioned migrations — one deployable unit, no external DB.

xgboost.name: XGBoost
xgboost.why: Drawdown-probability leg, walk-forward validated before its fusion weight opened.

shap.name: SHAP
shap.why: Per-feature attribution for the ML leg — association, not causation.

arch.name: arch (GJR-GARCH)
arch.why: Volatility forecasting with asymmetric shock response; HAR-RV alongside.

data.name: Twelve Data + akshare
data.why: US prices via a commercial API; China A-shares via akshare. Daily snapshots as fallback.

render.name: Render
render.why: Free-tier deploy; the cold-start banner exists because of it.
